risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." / "All women meeting the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to the treatment groups using the SurveyMonkey tool." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants were randomly assigned using a web-based tool, which is a clear context clue supporting a proper random allocation sequence. Although the paper does not specify the exact algorithm, the description is sufficient for low risk under a permissive Cochrane approach.

_evidence.method_quote: “The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool.” / “All women meeting the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to the treatment groups using the SurveyMonkey tool.”
_evidence.method_summary: Participants meeting inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to groups using the SurveyMonkey web-based tool.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." Comment: This describes a web-based randomization system, i.e. central allocation, which prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment. Although the article does not provide additional details such as envelopes or pharmacy control, the explicitly stated web-based allocation mechanism is sufficient for low risk of allocation concealment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool.
_evidence.note: The article describes randomisation with SurveyMonkey but does not provide additional details about concealment beyond web-based allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A three-armed randomised controlled trial was conducted, comparing 12 sessions of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) acupuncture in one group, 12 h of PFMT in a second group, including one individual consultation with a specialised physiotherapist before group training, and a waiting list control group..." Comment: The study does not report any participant or personnel blinding, and the extracted evidence explicitly indicates participants were not blinded and personnel were not blinded. Because the main outcomes were self-reported/subjective (ICIQ-UI SF, expectations of treatment effect, adverse events), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "ICIQ-UI SF is a well validated self-reporting outcome form"; "The questionnaires functioned adequately and all questions were filled in accordingly." Comment: The main outcome and adverse events/expectations were patient-reported, so the patient was the outcome assessor. Participants were not reported as blinded, and the study itself notes that "Higher expectations for the treatments were associated with higher outcome scores," indicating that knowledge/expectation could influence self-reported outcome measurement. Because at least one subjective outcome was assessed by unblinded participants, the worst-outcome rule makes the overall detection-bias judgement High risk.

Quote: "The number of dropouts was high in all groups: 50% in the waiting list group, 40% in the physiotherapy group, and 25% in the acupuncture group." Comment: The analysed endpoint had substantial post-randomization attrition (12 randomized vs 8 analysed in acupuncture; 10 vs 6 in physiotherapy; 12 vs 6 in waiting list), with overall loss 35% and a 25 percentage point imbalance between arms. Reasons were not fully benign: the text reports one acupuncture withdrawal due to "no effect" and one participant with cancer, and the waiting-list group had the highest dropout. Complete-case analysis was used with no imputation, so the missing outcome data could plausibly bias the endpoint results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “only two women withdrew during the study, one due to no effect, and the other due to cancer. A further two dropped out before treatment”

“Three of the four dropouts in the physiotherapy group dropped out before attending the first session, due to the inconvenient training time of 09:00. Another woman, aged 87 years, dropped out after her first session of PFMT because she was not able to exercise.”
“The number of dropouts was high in all groups: 50% in the waiting list group”

_evidence.notes: Losses are based on the primary clinical outcome time point at 12 weeks / endpoint; table 2 reports endpoint ICIQ-UI SF and the text reports dropout by group. The study describes completed questionnaires among those who continued, with no imputation mentioned.
